item.title: 559 Accelerated Honors Spring 2025 Announcement
item.description: Moved to the course web

item.title: Using a Distance Sensor to Detect Deviations in a Planar Surface
Miniature Time of Flight Sensors for Robot Perception
item.description:
Workshop webpage: 7th International Workshop on Visual Odometry and Computer Vision

A link to my talk on YouTube. (this should take you directly to my talk). This link should be after the intro (when I start talking).

The entire workshop is on YouTube. My talk starts at 7:12.

item.title: Towards 3D Vision with Low-Cost Single Photon Cameras
item.description: Overview of our research on reconstructing 3D objects via measurements from a distributed set of miniature time-of-flight sensors.

item.title: IKLink: End-Effector Trajectory Tracking with Minimal Reconfigurations
Ranged Kinematics and Time of Flight Sensors for Shared Autonomy Robotic Systems
item.description: <iframe src="https://www.slideshare.net/slideshow/embed_code/key/EyW1T5nxEtMucz?hostedIn=slideshare&page=upload" width="476" height="400" frameborder="0" marginwidth="0" marginheight="0" scrolling="no"></iframe>

item.title: Enhancing Text Corpus Exploration with Post Hoc Explanations and Comparative Design
item.description: This video shows off the AbstractsViewer system and its key ideas.

item.title: Novel Sensors for Robotics Applications
item.description: We are exploring how we can use emerging sensors in robotics applications. New sensors offer different tradeoffs and capabilities which provides opportunities for robotics usages. For example, we are working with Single Photon Avalanche Diode (SPAD) time of flight sensors which provide distance information in small, low-power packages. These sensors provide different information than more traditional ones: for example providing statistical distributions over an area, rather than detailed measurements.

item.title: Visualization Theory: Summarization, Uncertainty, ...
item.description: We are exploring very basic questions in how to present information with visualizations. We are examining the central concept of summarization to understand how people use summaries and what strategies can be used to create them. This leads to a broader concept of understanding how people use visualizations to ask and answer questions. We are trying to codify the process for creating effective visualizations to make it easier for designers.

item.title: Awareness of (and with) Robots
item.description: We are interested in how we can help human stakeholders (operators, observers, etc.) have appropriate understanding of robots and their situations. This requires us to design methods (such as visualizations) that help communicate robot state, environment, plans, and history to users. One aspect that we explore is using robots to provide viewpoints (move cameras) to help observe robots (or other aspects of the environments).

item.title: Shared Autonomy for Robotic Inspection
item.description: We are developing robot solutions to automate inspection, where a mobile robot with a set of sensors scans through a space. Such applications involve a human collaborator, to specify the task, to supervise operation, to assess the results, or to guide the robot through challenging aspects. We seek to develop systems that share control: automating as much as possible, but allowing for user contributions as necessary. This application project is driving techical work in motion synthesis, mobile sensing, and user experience.

item.title: Exploiting Task Tolerances in Mimicry-based Telemanipulation
Unlocking the Performance of Proximity Sensors by Utilizing Transient Histograms
RangedIK: An Optimization-based Robot Motion Generation Method for Ranged-Goal Tasks
Summarization, Uncertainty, Estimation… Models as a basis for visualization
Interacting with Models: Different Views of Visualization Tasks
Geometric Calibration of Single-Pixel Distance Sensors
Trinary Tools for Continuously Valued Classifiers (CBoxer Talk Video)
item.description:
This video accompanies our paper Trinary tools for continuously valued binary classifiers about the CBoxer system. This video is the “talk” from the online VisXAI workshop that was part of Pacific Vis 2022.

For more information see the Boxer Project Page.

item.title: Trinary Tools for Continuously Valued Classifiers (Paper Accompaniment Video)
item.description:
This video accompanies our paper Trinary tools for continuously valued binary classifiers about the CBoxer system.

For more information see the Boxer Project Page.

item.title: Understanding Control Frames in Multi-Camera Robot Telemanipulation (talk video)
Student Start
item.description:
If you are working with me (even as a directed study student), you will need to do some things for the University and my funding agencies.

I ask that everyone do these things, even if at first they may not be required.

  1. Before you can start officially, you must complete “Responsible Conduct of Research” Training. https://research.wisc.edu/integrity-and-other-requirements/responsible-conduct-of-research-rcr/

    I think it’s a bunch of Canvas quizzes. It shouldn’t take long. Please do it ASAP.

item.title: Interpretting Embeddings With Comparison
item.description:
Invited Keynote at the CSIG-VIS International Lecture Series (China, virtual).

A recording of the talk is available at: https://www.bilibili.com/video/BV1Hv411u7c6

Interpreting Embeddings with Comparison from gleicher

item.title: Informing Real-Time Corrections in Corrective Shared Autonomy Through Expert Demonstrations
item.description: Video accompaniment to the RAL/IROS 2021 Paper Informing Real-Time Corrections in Corrective Shared Autonomy Through Expert Demonstrations.

item.title: Corrective Shared Autonomy for Addressing Task Variability
item.description: Video accompaniment to the RAL/ICRA 2021 Paper Corrective Shared Autonomy for Addressing Task Variability.

item.title: Recognizing Orientation Slip in Human Demonstrations
item.description: Video accompaniment to the ICRA 2021 Paper Recognizing Orientation Slip in Human Demonstrations.

item.title: Single-query Path Planning Using Sample-efficient Probability Informed Trees
CollisionIK: A Per-Instant Method for Generating Robot Motions with Environment Collision Avoidance
Strobe: An Acceleration Meta-algorithm for Optimizing Robot Paths using Concurrent Interleaved Pods
Reference Letters for Students in Classes
item.description:
I will not provide reference letters for students that I know only through a class (as a student).

I get many requests from students from my classes to provide reference (recommendation) letters for graduate school applications. I will not provide these letters.

I will provide letters for students that I get to know outside of regular classes. For example, if you are involved in a directed study project or work with me as a peer mentor for a class (that’s why it says “as a student”).

Search Results

Waiting for search script to run...

item.title: Undergrad Research, Projects and Directed Studies
item.description:
If you are currently an undergraduate and interested in getting involved in a project, please read this. A lot of students seem to be interested in doing “research” these days, and I’d like to set the expectations correctly.

Short version: (1) understand research vs. projects; (2) don’t do research / directed study until you’ve done the requisite coursework; (3) I will say no if you are not a Wisconsin student; (4) consider things to build your portfolio on your own.

There’s also the general question of “I am interested in the topics you are interested in, how do I learn more about them”. If you’re interested in graphics, robotics, or data vis there is some on that here too.

item.title: Test Title
item.description: Test Body.

item.title: Video, Animation and Image Authoring
item.description: Our goal is to make it easier for people to create useable images and video. For example, we have developed methods for improving pictures and video as a post-process (e.g. removing shadows and stabilizing video). We have also worked on adapting imagery for use in new settings (e.g. image and video retargeting or automatic video editing) and making use of large image collections (e.g. intestingness detection or panorama finding).

item.title: Visualizing English Print
item.description: To drive our data science efforts, we took a specific application: working with English literature scholars to develop approaches to working with large collections of historical texts.

item.title: Perceptual Principles for Visualization
item.description: Understanding how people see can inform how we should design visualizations. We have been exploring how recent results in perception (e.g., ensemble encoding) can be exploited to create novel visualization designs, and how principles of perception can inform visualization designs.

item.title: Communicative Characters
item.description: We are working on better ways to synthesize human motions to make animated characters (both on screen and robots) that are better able to communicate. Generally, we focus on trying to make use of collections of examples (such as motion capture) to build models that allow us to generate novel movements, or to define models of communicative motions.

item.title: Usable VR and AR
item.description: Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) are interesting display devices that are becoming practical. We are exploring how to design VR and AR applications that can address application tasks, as well as to develop new mechanisms that will make these displays more useful across a broad range of applications.

item.title: Visualizing Comparisons for Data Science
item.description: Data interpretation tasks often involve making comparisons among the data, or can be thought of as comparisons. We are developing better visualization tools for performing comparisons for various data challenges, as well as to developing better methods for inventing new designs.

item.title: Interacting with Machine Learning
item.description: People interact with machine learning systems in many ways: they must build them, debug them, diagnose them, decide to trust them, gain insights on their data from them, etc. We are exploring this in both directions: How do we build machine learning tools into interactive data analysis in order to help people interpret large and complex data? How do we build interaction tools that can help people construct and diagnose machine learning models?

item.title: Bad Page
item.description: This is a placeholder for old pages that did not get converted.

item.title: Communicating Physical Interactions
item.description: We are working on ways for people and robots to communicate to each other about how objects should be manipulated in the world. Manipulations necessarily involve physical interactions (e.g., forces must be applied correctly). We are exploring ways for people to tell robots how to act with appropriate forces (e.g., to teach manipulation skills) as well as for robots to communicate back to people about the actions they are performing.

item.title: Boxer: Interactive Comparison of Classifiers (Talk Video)
item.description:
This video was the “talk” for EuroVis 2020 that presented our paper Boxer: Interactive Comparison of Classifier Results. Because EuroVis was held virtually (due to COVID-19), the talk is this 12 minute video.

For more information see the Boxer Project Page.

item.title: Boxer: Interactive Comparison of Classifiers (Paper Video)
item.description: See also the talk video.

item.title: Dumb Robots for Smart People
item.description:
Invited Keynote at the AI-HRI Symposium (AAAI Fall Symposium Series)

Dumb Robots for Smart People (Direct Control Interfaces for Robotics) from gleicher

item.title: About Michael Gleicher (Bio)
item.description:
(or look at my C.V.)

One paragraph standard professional bio

One paragraph profession bio - 2023

Michael Gleicher is a Professor in the Department of Computer Sciences at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. His research interests span the range of visual computing, including data visualization, robotics, and virtual reality. Current projects focus on semi-autonomous robotic systems and presentation of complex data. Prof. Gleicher is founder of the Department’s Visual Computing Group and co-directs both the Visual Computing Laboratory and the Collaborative Robotics Laboratory at UW-Madison. His work explores challenges in creating systems that work well for people. His work has been supported by NSF, NASA, Los Alamos National Laboratories, DARPA, NIH, Mellon Foundation, and others. His work has received numerous awards in conferences including IEEE Visualization and IEEE/ACM Human-Robot Interaction. He has been Papers chair for EuroVis and Area Chair for IEEE Vis. Prior to joining the University, Prof. Gleicher was a researcher at The Autodesk Vision Technology Center and in Apple Computer’s Advanced Technology Group. He earned his Ph. D. in Computer Science (1994) from Carnegie Mellon University, and holds a B.S.E. in Electrical Engineering from Duke University (1988). In 2013-2014, he was a visiting researcher at INRIA Rhone-Alpes. Prof. Gleicher is an ACM Distinguished Scientist.

item.title: Remote Telemanipulation with Adapting Viewpoints in Visually Complex Environments
Shared-Control-Based Bimanual Robot Manipulation
item.description: This video demonstrates our system for interactive control of bimanual robots, published in Science Robotics, June 2019.

item.title: Stampede: A Discrete-Optimization Method for Solving Pathwise-Inverse Kinematics
User-Guided Offline Synthesis of Robot Arm Motion from 6-DoF Paths
Characterizing Input Methods for Human-to-robot Demonstrations
Interpreting Embeddings with Comparison
item.description: Invited talk at the Computer Science Department Seminar, University of Arizona, Tuscon, January 15, 2019.

item.title: What Shakespeare Taught Us About Visualization and Data Science
item.description: Invited talk at the TRIPODS (Data Science) Seminar, University of Arizona, Tuscon, January 14, 2019. This talk is similar to the previous talk of the same title.

item.title: Inferring Geometric Constraints in Human Demonstrations
Robust Replay of Human Demonstrations Using Identified Constraints
item.description: This video went along with a paper that was never published (it was submitted to ICRA ‘19). The methods are described in Guru’s Thesis. The constraint inference approach is discussed in this paper on Axiv.

item.title: RelaxedIK: Real-time Synthesis of Accurate and Feasible Robot Arm Motion
Interactive Analysis of Word Vector Embeddings
item.description: Paper presentation at EuroVis 2018

item.title: Perception, Comparison, and Models for Uncertainty
item.description: Invited talk at the EuroRV^3 Uncertainty workshop

item.title: What Shakespeare Taught Us About Visualization and Data Science
item.description: Invited talk at the University of Vienna, May 30, 2018. This talk is similar to the previous talk of the same title.

item.title: An Autonomous Dynamic Camera Method for Effective Remote Teleoperation
item.description:
This is the video accompaniment to our best-paper award winning paper An Autonomous Dynamic Camera Method for Effective Remote Teleoperation from HRI 2018. It demonstrates a system that allows the user to control a robot manipulator and see what they are doing by having a second robot control a video camera that watches the action.

See also the later video from the system that added more intelligence to the camera control, published at RSS 2019.

item.title: What Shakespeare Taught Us About Visualization and Data Science
item.description: Invited keynote at the IEEE Professional Communications Society Meeting

item.title: Through the Lens of 25 Years: Through-the-Lens Camera Control Revisited
item.description: Invited Keynote at WICED 2017 (6th Workshop on Intelligent Cinematography and Editing), Lyon, France.

item.title: A Motion Retargeting Method for Effective Mimicry-based Teleoperation of Robot Arms
What Shakespeare Taught Us About Data Science and Visualization
item.description: Seminar at Johns Hopkins University, December 2016. A similar talk was given at New York University in October.

item.title: Authoring Directed Gaze for Full-body Motion Capture
Towards Comprehensible Modeling (Human-Centric Issues in Data Analytics)
item.description: Talk at the Midwest Big Data Workshop at the University of Chicago.

item.title: Advice on Talks
item.description: I have written some advice on how to give a talk - that is the basis for a lecture in my grad classes.

item.title: Visualization (Tutorial at EMDA)
item.description:
An invited tutorial on Visualization at the Early Modern Digital Agendas workshop at the Folger Library, Washington, DC.

  • 1 - Intro
  • 2 - What is Visualization?
  • 3 - Encodings

item.title: Seeing, Scatterplots and Shakespeare
item.description: Invited talk at Portland State University, May 2015. This talk is an update of the earlier talk with a similar title.

item.title: Serendip Rank Viewer
item.description: A demonstration of Serendip’s RankViewer. Serendip is a corpus exploration tool that uses probabilistic topic modeling as a lens through which to view documents. The demonstration uses a topic model generated on a corpus of 1,080 digitized English texts published between 1530 and 1799.

item.title: Towards Comprehensible Predictive Modeling
item.description: Talk at the VisWeek 2014 Workshop on Visualization for Predictive Analytics

item.title: Towards Comprehensible Modeling
item.description: Talk at the Dagstuhl Seminar “Scientific Visualization”, June 2, 2014

item.title: Seeing, Scatterplots and Shakespeare: Adventures in Data Visualization
item.description: Talk given at the Journée scientifique de l’ARC 6 in Grenoble. This was a meeting of researchers from various fields from around the Rhone-Alpes region. (slides 9-up) This talk was also given at the Technische Universitat Wien (Vienna, Austria, December 2013) and INRAI-Saclay (near Paris, February 2014), with very similar slides.

item.title: Explainers: Expert Explorations with Crafted Projections
item.description: Talk given at VIS 2013 conference for a VAST (TVCG) paper.

item.title: Splatterplots: Overcoming Overdraw in Scatter Plots
item.description: Talk given at VIS 2013 conference for a TVCG journal paper.

from Art and Perception to Visualization, Video, and Virtual Reality

item.title: Seeing Shakespeare (and Sequences): Making pictures to learn from stuff you don't want to read

item.title: Effective Replays and Summarization of Virtual Experiences
From Art and Perception to Visualization and Video
item.description: A talk at the CMU Robotics seminar. A video of the talk is on YouTube (availble in HD)!

item.title: Pictures from Piles of Data: How Graphics, Multimedia, Vision, Visualization, Animation and Cartography All Connect
item.description: A Talk at the Institute for Mathematics and its Applications (IMA) at the University of Minnesota.

item.title: SubSpace Video Stabilization (ToG 2011)
item.description:
Video from our 2011 Transactions on Graphics Paper.

This system provided a practical approach to warp stabilization. It is the key technical piece that lead to Adobe’s Warp Stabilizer.

This work was done with Feng Liu, Michael Gleicher, Jue Wang, Hailin Jin, and Aseem Agarwala.

item.title: Using Perception to Make Better Pictures
item.description: A Talk at the ERI seminar, University of Wisconsin, Madison (note, this is a shortened version of the Pictures from Piles of Data talk)

item.title: Pictures from Piles of Data: How Graphics, Multimedia, Vision, Visualization, Animation and Cartography All Connect
item.description: A Talk at the Chaos and Complexity seminar, University of Wisconsin, Madison

item.title: A Conversation About Visualization
item.description: A talk at The Hacker Within monthly meeting.

item.title: Pictures from Piles of Data: How Graphics, Multimedia, Vision, Visualization, Animation and Cartography All Connect
Content-Preserving Warps for 3D Video Stabilization
item.description: This is a video demonstration from our SIGGRAPH 2009 paper “Content-Preserving Warps for 3D Video Stabilization”. This is an early predecessor to the technology that became Adobe’s Warp Stabilizer (although, that is really based on a later paper).

item.title: Discovering Panoramas in Web Videos
item.description: Presentation at ACM Multimedia 2008

item.title: Motion Synthesis by Example: Data-Centric Approches for Animating Game Characters
item.description: Distinguished Lecture at the University of Utah Computer Sciences Department. Note: because of the slide animation, some of this doesn’t look so great in print form.

item.title: Synthesis by Example: Connecting Motion Planning and Example-Based Movement
item.description: Presentation at SIGGRAPH 2008 course “Motion Planning and Autonomy for Virtual Humans.”

item.title: More Motion Capture* in Games: Can we make example-based approaches scale
item.description: Presentation at the Motion in Games Workshop, Utrecht, June 2008.

item.title: Re-Cinematography (2007)
item.description:
This video demonstrates Re-Cinematography - our approach for post-process video stabilization that tries to produce high-quality camera motions. The original paper (at MultiMedia 2007) won the “best in track award,” and was invited to be extended as a journal paper in TOMCCAP.

Note: this is taken from a “FLV” file so, at one point it was highly compressed using 2007 technology. It was before we had reliable H.264 (or better).

item.title: Re-Cinematography: Improving the Camera Dynamics of Casual Video
item.description: Presentation at ACM Multimedia 2007 in the Best Papers Session.

item.title: Parametric Motion Graphs
item.description: We present an example-based motion synthesis technique that generates continuous streams of high-fidelity, controllable motion for interactive applications, such as video games.

item.title: Virtual Videography
item.description:
We present automated system called Virtual Videography that employs the art of videography to mimic videographer-produced lecture videos, while being unobtrusive when recording.

The system is described in a 2007 Paper.

item.title: Splicing Upper-Body Actions with Locomotion
item.description: We present a simple and efficient technique for synthesizing high-fidelity motions by attaching, or splicing,the upper-body action of one motion example to the lower-body locomotion of another.

item.title: Human Motion Synthesis by Example
item.description: A Talk Given at the Game Technologies Seminar. This is really the first part of a tag-team talk given with Lucas. Originally it was scheduled for a longer period of time, so most of the slides were skipped in order to cram it into 75 minutes.

item.title: Automated Extraction and Parameterization of Motions in Large Data Sets
item.description: We provide automated methods for identifying logically similar motions in a data set and using them to build a continuous and intuitively parameterized space of motions.

item.title: Animation by Example
item.description: An overview talk given at both Seoul National University and KAIST (Korean Advanced Institute for Science and Technology).

item.title: Animation by Example 1: Introduction and Motion Representation
item.description: Lecture given at the Workshop on Image Processing and Related Issues at Zhejiang University. This lecture was the first 2 parts of 5 (it was given as a 2 hour lecture with a break in the middle).

item.title: Animation by Example 2: Motion Signal Processing
item.description: Lecture given at the Workshop on Image Processing and Related Issues at Zheziang University. This lecture was the 3 part of 5.

item.title: Animation by Example 4: Concatenative Approaches
item.description: Lecture given at the Workshop on Image Processing and Related Issues at Zhejiang University. This lecture was part 4 of 5.

item.title: Animation by Example 5: Parametric Methods
item.description: Lecture given at the Workshop on Image Processing and Related Issues at Zhejiang University. This lecture was part 5 of 5.

item.title: Virtual Videography 2004 (early result)
item.description: Note: see the Virtual Videography video page for a more recent result.

item.title: Imaging and Visualizing Microvascular Architecture
item.description: Seminar presented at the CIBM seminar series, November 11, 2003. Talk split with co-PI Garet Lahvis.

item.title: The Graduate School FAQ
item.description: This is advice for undergraduates who are thinking about going to graduate school. There is specific advice for students who are applying to Wisconsin, and even more specifically, might like to work with me.

item.title: Building Efficient, Accurate Character Skins from Examples
Flexible Automatic Motion Blending with Registration Curves
Motion Editing with Paths and Tiles
item.description: This video shows off work we did in 2003, but was never published. The student graduated before we were able to complete the paper.

item.title: Direct Manipulation of Interactive Character Skins
Snap Together Motion: Assembling Run-Time Animation
item.description: We present an approach to character motion called Snap-Together Motion that addresses the unique demands of virtual environments. Snap-Together Motion (STM) preprocesses a corpus of motion capture examples into a set of short clips that can be concatenated to make continuous streams of motion.

item.title: Human Animation for Interactive Systems
item.description: This is a talk given at the University of Texas, Austin and at Sony (August 2003).

item.title: Virtual Videography (2003 early version)
item.description: The main idea behind Virtual Videography is to automatically edit video of some event that would be nice to record, but too costly or intrusive to place a camera crew, such as a classroom lecture. (Note: Newer video at Virtual Videography).

item.title: Animation By Example
item.description: Seminar presented various places (including Illinois, IBM, Stanford, Pixar, Michigan, Penn)

item.title: Footskate Cleanup for Motion Capture Editing
item.description:
Video from our 2002 paper “Footskate cleanup for motion capture editing”. This work was published at the Symposium on Computer Animation.

Many motion capture editing operations result in the feet of the character moving when they ought to remain planted. We present a simple, efficient algorithm for removing this footskate.

item.title: HijackGL: Reconstructing from Streams for Stylized Rendering
Evaluating Video-Based Motion Capture
item.description: Presented at Computer Animation 2002

item.title: GL-HiJacking: Reconstructing from Streams for Stylized Rendering
item.description: Presented at NPAR 2002

item.title: Computer Animation: Its Past, Its Present, And its Future at UW
item.description: Talk for the CS Club to introduce what we do at Wisconsin.

item.title: Animation by Adaptation: Tales of Motion Use and Re-Use
item.description: Presented at Advanced Center for Computing in the Arts and Design,Ohio State University, July 2001

item.title: Animation by Adaptation: Motion Editing
item.description: Presented at the Computer Graphics Tutorial at the Institute for Mathematics and Its Applications at the University of Minnesota.

item.title: Quack! Quick! We need a Dancing Duck! Tales of Motion Use and Re-Use
item.description: Presented at the Computer Graphics Workshop at the Institute for Mathematics and Its Applications.

item.title: Motion Path Editing
item.description: Presented at the 2001 Symposium on Interactive 3D graphics

item.title: Motion Path Editing
item.description: Warning: this is a poor quality AVI upload.

item.title: Animation by Adaptation
item.description: Distinguished Lecture, Department of Computer Science, University of Virginia

item.title: Status Reports
item.description: If you work for me (for example, as an RA or an undergrad directed study) you must send me a status report every week.

item.title: Retargeting Motion to New Characters
item.description: SIGGRAPH ‘98 talk (both left and right slide decks in one file)

item.title: Editing and Retargetting Animated Motion with Spacetime Constraints
item.description: My 1998 Job Talk (good enough to get me a job at Wisconsin!)

item.title: Retargeting Motion to New Characters
item.description: I3D 1997 talk (scanned from 35mm slides)

item.title: Motion Editing With Spacetime Constraints
item.description:
Yes. This really was all in real-time on a circa 1996 Macintosh. I have no idea how I made it go so fast.

This video is from the 1997 Interactive 3D Symposium Paper. An earlier version of the paper actually appeared as a 1998 journal paper. The journal paper mainly has 2D examples, and focuses on the non-interactive cases.

item.title: A Differential Approach to Graphical Manipulation

item.description: This should redirect from Mike Gleicher's Home Page to the Graphics Group Papers Page with an appropriate search: https://graphics.cs.wisc.edu/Papers/?author=Gleicher

item.title: 2009 Home Page
item.description: This is an Ancient (2009) version of my home page. Page last modified on July 16, 2009, at 11:52 AM.

item.title: 2013 Graphics Qual - Main Reading List
item.description: 2013 Graphics Qual - main reading list.

item.title: 2013 Qual: Readings from Foundations of Computer Graphics
item.description: Textbook readings - materials from an undergrad class.

item.title: 2013 Qual: Real-Time Rendering
item.description: 2013 Graphics Qual - readings from the Real Time Rendering book.

item.title: 2013 Qual: Surface Parameterization
item.description: 2013 Graphics Qual - readings on surface parameterization.

item.title: 2013 Qual: Textbooks
item.description: Textbook reading for the 2013 Graphics Qual.

item.title: Advice on Prelims and Dissertations (Theses)
item.description: I have pretty strong opinions on what a thesis should be. My opinion might matter to you (if you want me to sign off), but they might be a generally good way to think about writing anything.

item.title: advice-bibliographies
item.description:
Reading is an important part of doing research.

Part of the problem is keeping track of what you’ve read. I strongly recommend that you keep a bibliography (database) of everything you read that is research/work related.

Update: back when I wrote this, there weren’t any good technological solutions to this. That has changed a bit. As of 2020, I am using Zotero. Medeley is in some ways a better alternative, but there are issues that the company that runs it treats your data in an unacceptable manner.

item.title: Bad Page Request
item.description:
The URL that you were looking for was on my old web site.

In Summer of 2020, I began the project of rebuilding my website. Almost all pages from the old site have been recreated, but there are not necessarily redirects to the new pages.

You’ll have to look around a bit to find out where things have gone.

item.title: courses-games10announce
item.description: This is a historic announcement for the 2010 Computer Games Class. It was the first time I taught the class.

item.title: Pre-2010 Papers By Topic
item.description:
Selected Historical Publications (Papers, Talks, Videos), Organized by Topic

This page has not been updated since 2010! Please See http://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~gleicher/Papers for an up to date list!

This list is not necessarily complete, and hasn’t been updated in quite some time. For a more complete list, check the automatically generated lists of papers, talks, or videos. This is mainly things from the past few years.

1. Visualization and Protein Science

1.1 Bioinformatics Visualizations (Sequences)

1.2 Protein Visualization

  • Aaron Bryden, George Phillips Jr., Michael Gleicher. Automated Illustration of Molecular Flexibility. IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics. (page) (pdf)
  • Greg Cipriano Michael Gleicher. Molecular Surface Abstraction. IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics (Proceedings Visualization 2007). October 2007. PDF EE/DOI
  • Aneesh Karve and Michael Gleicher. Glyph-based Overviews of Large Datasets in Structural Bioinformatics. _ Proc. 11th Intl. Conf. on Information Visualization (IV 2007)._ July 2007. PDF EE/DOI

1.3 Other Visualization Stuff

  • Greg Cipriano Michael Gleicher. Text Scaffolds for Effective Surface Labeling. IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics (Proceedings Visualization 2008). October 2008. PDF EE/DOI
  • Tom Brunet K. Evan Nowak Michael Gleicher. Integrating Dynamic Deformations into Interactive Volume Visualization. Proceedings Eurographics/IEEE-VCTC Symposium on Visualization (EuroVis) 2006. May 2006. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher Tom Brunet Evan Nowak Elizabeth Osten Mathew McElwee Kevin Tanty Adam Gepner Garet Lahvis. Capillary Histology Imagery Visualization and Exploration. Proceedings of IEEE Visualization 04 (Poster Session). October 2004. PDF

2. Multimedia

2.1 Image and Video Retargeting

  • Yanwen Guo, Feng Liu, Jian Shi, Zhihua Zhou and Michael Gleicher.. Image Retargeting Using Mesh Parametrization. IEEE Transactions on Multimedia. 2009. (pdf)
  • Feng Liu and Michael Gleicher. Video Retargeting: Automating Pan-and-Scan. ACM Multimedia 2006. October 2006. PDF EE/DOI
  • Vidya Setlur and Saeko Takagi and Ramesh Raskar and Michael Gleicher and Bruce Gooch. Automatic Image Retargeting. Proceedings of 4th International Conference on Mobile and Ubiquitous Multimedia. October 2005. EE/DOI
  • Feng Liu and Michael Gleicher. Automatic Image Retargeting with Fisheye-View Warping. Proceedings of ACM UIST 2005. October 2005. PDF

2.2 Camera Motion Improvement

  • Feng Liu, Michael Gleicher, Hailin Jin and Aseem Agarwala. Content-Preserving Warps for 3D Video Stabilization. ACM Transactions on Graphics (Proceedings of SIGGRAPH 2009). Aug. 2009. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher and Feng Liu. Re-Cinematography: Improving the Camerawork of Casual Video. ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing Communications and Applications (TOMCCAP), 5, 1, 2. October 2008. PDF EE/DOI
  • Michael Gleicher and Feng Liu. Re-Cinematography: Improving the Camera Dynamics of Casual Video. ACM Multimedia 2007, best paper nominee. September 2007. PDF EE/DOI

2.3 Image and Video Editing

  • Feng Liu and Michael Gleicher. Texture-Consistent Shadow Removal. ECCV 2008. Oct. 2008. PDF EE/DOI
  • Feng Liu, Jinjun Wang, Shenghuo Zhu, Michael Gleicher and Yihong Gong. Noisy Video Super-Resolution. ACM Multimedia 2008 (short paper). Oct. 2008. PDF EE/DOI

2.4 Automatic Video Production

  • Rachel Heck Michael Wallick Michael Gleicher. Virtual Videography. ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications. February 2007. PDF EE/DOI
  • Rachel Heck, Michael Wallick and Michael Gleicher. Virtual Videography (video abstract). ACM Multimedia 2006 (Video Program). October 2006. PDF
  • Michael Wallick, Rachel Heck, and Michael Gleicher. Chalkboard and Marker Regions. Proceedings of Mirage 2005. March 2005. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher Rachel Heck Michael Wallick. A Framework for Virtual Videography. __ Smart Graphics. June 2002. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher and James Masanz. Towards Virtual Videography. __ ACM Multimedia 2000, Los Angeles, CA. November, 2000. PDF

2.5 Image and Video Analysis

  • Feng Liu, Yuzhen Niu and Michael Gleicher. Using Web Images for Measuring Video Frame Interestingness. IJCAI 2009. July, 2009. (pdf)
  • Feng Liu and Michael Gleicher. Learning color and locality cues for moving object detection and segmentation. IEEE CVPR. Jun. 2009. PDF

3. Image and Video Databases

  • Feng Liu, Yuhen-Hu and Michael Gleicher. Discovering Panoramas in Web Videos. ACM Multimedia 2008. Oct. 2008. PDF EE/DOI
  • Feng Liu and Michael Gleicher. Region enhanced scale-invariant saliency detection. Proceedings of IEEE ICME 2006. July 2006. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Projective Registration with Difference Decomposition. Proceedings of CVPR ‘97. June 1997. PDF

4. Animation

4.1 Motion Synthesis, Capture and Editing

  • Patrick Coleman, Jacobo Bibliowicz, Karan Singh, and Michael Gleicher. Staggered Poses: A Character Motion Representation for Detail-Preserving Editing of Pose and Coordinated Timing. Proceedings of 2008 Symposium on Computer Animation. July 2008. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Graph-based motion synthesis: an annotated bibliography. SIGGRAPH Course. July 2008. Note: May be updated from time to time. Check the PDF here, not the official ACM version. EE/DOI
  • Michael Gleicher. Autonomous navigation for a virtual human - part I. SIGGRAPH Course. July 2008. EE/DOI
  • Michael Gleicher. More Motion Capture in Games – Can We Make Example-Based Approaches Scale?. Motion in Games: First International Workshop, MIG 2008, Utrecht, The Netherlands, June 14-17, 2008. Revised Papers. Springer LNCS.. June 2008. EE/DOI
  • Rachel Heck Michael Gleicher. Parametric Motion Graphs. Proceedings of Symposium on Interactive 3D Graphics and Games (I3D) 2007. April 2007. PDF EE/DOI
  • Rachel Heck and Michael Gleicher. Parametric Motion Graphs. 2006 Symposium on Computer Animation (Poster Presentation). September 2006. PDF
  • Rachel Heck, Lucas Kovar and Michael Gleicher. Splicing Upper-Body Actions with Locomotion. Computer Graphics Forum (Proceedings Eurographics) - winner of 2nd prize best paper award. September 2006. PDF
  • Mankyu Sung, Locas Kovar, and Michael Gleicher. Fast and Accurate Goal-Directed Motion Synthesis for Crowds. Proceedings of 2005 Symposium on Computer Animation. July 2005. PDF
  • Lucas Kovar and Michael Gleicher. Automated Extraction and Parameterization of Motions in Large Data Sets. Proceedings SIGGRAPH 2004.. August 2004. PDF
  • Hyun Joon Shin, Lucas Kovar, and Michael Gleicher. Physical Touch-up of Human Motions. Pacific Graphics 2003. October 2003. PDF
  • Lucas Kovar and Michael Gleicher. Flexible Automatic Motion Blending with Registration Curves. Proceedings of 2003 Symposium on Computer Animation. July 2003. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher, Hyun Joon Shin, Lucas Kovar, and Andrew Jepsen. Snap Together Motion: Assembling Run-Time Animation. 2003 Symposium on Interactive 3D Graphics. April 2003. PDF
  • Lucas Kovar John Schreiner Michael Gleicher. Footskate Cleanup for Motion Capture Editing. Proceedings of the 2002 ACM Symposium on Computer Animation (SCA). July 2002. PDF
  • Lucas Kovar Michael Gleicher Frederic Pighin. Motion Graphs. ACM Transactions on Graphics 21(3) (Proceedings of SIGGRAPH 2002). July 2002. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher and Nicola Ferrier. Evaluating Video-Based Motion Capture. Proceedings of Computer Animation 2002. June 2002. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Comparing Constraint-Based Motion Editing Methods. Graphical Models. August 2001. PDF
  • Hyun Joon Shin, Jehee Lee, Michael Gleicher, and Sung Yong Shin. Computer Puppetry: An Importance-Based Approach. ACM Transactions of Graphics. April 2001. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Motion Path Editing. Proceedings of the 2001 ACM Symposium on Interactive 3D Graphics. March 2001. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Comparative Analysis of Contraint-Based Motion Editing Methods. __ 2000 Workshop on Human Modeling and Animation, Seoul Korea. June 2000. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Animation from Observation: Motion Capture and Modtion Editing. Computer Graphics. 33(4), 51-55. November, 1999. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher and Peter Litwinowicz. Constraint-Based Motion Adaptation. The Journal of Visualization and Computer Animation. 9, 65-94. 1998. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Retargetting Motion to New Characters. Proceedings of SIGGRAPH 98. In Computer Graphics Annual Conferance Series. 1998. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Motion Editing with Spacetime Constraints. Proceedings of the 1997 Symposium on Interactive 3D Graphics. PDF

4.2 Crowds

  • Mankyu Sung, Michael Gleicher, and Stephen Chenney. Scalable Behaviors for Crowd Simulation. Proceedings of Eurographics 2004. September 2004. PDF

4.3 Character Geometry (Skinning)

  • Alex Mohr Michael Gleicher. Building Efficient, Accurate Character Skins from Examples. ACM Transactions on Graphics 21(3) (Proceedings of SIGGRAPH 2003). July 2003. PDF
  • Yin Li, Michael Gleicher, Ying-Qinq Xu, and Harry Shum. Stylizing Motion with Drawings. Proceedings of 2003 Symposium on Computer Animation. July 2003. PDF
  • Alex Mohr, Luke Tokheim, and Michael Gleicher. Direct Manipulation of Interactive Character Skins. 2003 Symposium on Interactive 3D Graphics. April 2003. PDF

5. Other Graphics Things

  • Alex Mohr and Michael Gleicher. HijackGL: Reconstructing from Streams for Stylized Rendering. Proceedings of NPAR 2002. June 2002. PDF
  • Lucas Kovar and Michael Gleicher. Simplicial Families of Drawings. Proceedings UIST ‘01. November 2001. PDF
  • Alex Mohr and Michael Gleicher. Non-Invasive, Interactive, Stylized Rendering. Proceedings of the 2001 ACM Symposium on Interactive 3D Graphics. March 2001. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. Image Snapping. Proceedings of SIGGRAPH 95. Computer Graphics Annual Conference Series. August, 1995. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher and Andrew Witkin. Drawing with Contraints. The Visual Computer. 11(1):39-51. 1994. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher. A Graphics Toolkit Based on Differential Constraints. Proceedings UIST 93. November 1993. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher and Andrew Witkin. Supperting Numerical Computations in Interactive Contexts. Proceedings Graphics Interface 93. pp. 138-145. May 1993. PDF
  • Michael Gleicher and Andrew Witkin. Through-the-Lens Camera Control. Computer Graphics. 26:2, pp. 331-340. Processdings of SIGGRAPH 92. July 1992. PDF
  • Andrew Witkin, Michael Gleicher, and William Welch. Interactive Dynamics. Computer Graphics. 24(2):11-21. Proceedings 1990 Symposium on Interactive 3D Graphics. March 1990. PDF

item.title: Re-cinematography
item.description:
Re-Cinematography was our circa 2007 video stabilization system. At the time, it was way better than anything else. It inspired later techniques, notably our 2009 3D Warping technique, and our 2010 Subspace Warping technique (which evolved into the Adobe Warp Stabilizer.).

Re-Cinematography pushed the limits of what could be done with 2D global warps (using a single, 8 parameter projective warp over the entire image). The later techniques (e.g. SubSpace Video Stabilization (ToG 2011)) went beyond it by allowing for warping the image, not just global deformations.

item.title: Visualization 2010 Course Announcement
item.description: This is the historic announcement for the first time I offered a visualization class in 2010. It was an experiment that keeps going to this day (2020 as I write this). It’s pretty interesting that even at the beginning, I had the same ideas as to the goals of the class.

item.title: Write-Ups for Directed Study Projects (699s)
item.description: Making a final writeup for your directed study project (or summer intern project, or honors thesis project, or …) is important. Reflecting on what you’ve done is an important part of the learning process.

item.title: Writing Advice: The Paper "Recipe"
item.description: Let me tell you a little trick that I tell all the students: sentence, paragraph, page
